🪟 Windows Setup Guide

This documentation teaches Linux/Bash scripting (industry standard for DevOps). Windows users have several options to follow along.

Setting Up Your Environment on Windows

Choose your preferred approach for running bash scripts

🔧 Windows Options Comparison 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WINDOWS OPTIONS FOR BASH/LINUX                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   OPTION           │ BEST FOR           │ SETUP TIME            │
│   ─────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────────── │
│   WSL 2 ⭐         │ Full Linux exp.    │ 10-15 minutes         │
│   Git Bash         │ Basic bash         │ 5 minutes             │
│   PowerShell       │ Windows-native     │ Already installed     │
│   Docker           │ Isolated envs      │ 15-20 minutes         │
│                                                                  │
│   ⭐ RECOMMENDED: WSL 2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux)           │
│   • Full Linux environment inside Windows                        │
│   • Run all bash scripts natively                               │
│   • Access to apt, docker, and all Linux tools                  │
│   • Integrates with VS Code                                      │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Option 1: WSL 2 Installation (Recommended) Full Linux environment on Windows
▶️ PowerShell (Run as Administrator)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Install WSL with Ubuntu (one command!)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

wsl --install

# This installs:
# - WSL 2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux)
# - Ubuntu (default Linux distribution)
# - Virtual Machine Platform

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: Restart your computer when prompted
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# After restart, Ubuntu will open automatically
# Create your Linux username and password

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Verify installation (in PowerShell)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

wsl --list --verbose
# Should show: Ubuntu Running 2

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4: Update Ubuntu (in WSL terminal)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Open Ubuntu from Start menu, then run:
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# OPTIONAL: Install additional distros
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

wsl --list --online              # See available distros
wsl --install -d Debian          # Install Debian
wsl --install -d kali-linux      # Install Kali Linux
▶️ VS Code Integration
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INSTALL VS CODE WSL EXTENSION
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# 1. Open VS Code
# 2. Install "WSL" extension (ms-vscode-remote.remote-wsl)
# 3. Click green button bottom-left → "Connect to WSL"

# OR from WSL terminal:
code .                           # Opens VS Code in current directory

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ACCESS WINDOWS FILES FROM WSL
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Windows C: drive is mounted at /mnt/c
cd /mnt/c/Users/YourName/Desktop

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ACCESS WSL FILES FROM WINDOWS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# In Windows Explorer address bar:
# \\wsl$\Ubuntu\home\username
Option 2: Git Bash (Lightweight) Basic bash for simple scripts
▶️ Installation
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INSTALL GIT FOR WINDOWS (includes Git Bash)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Option A: Download from https://git-scm.com/download/win

# Option B: Using winget (Windows Package Manager)
winget install Git.Git

# Option C: Using Chocolatey
choco install git

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# LAUNCH GIT BASH
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# - Right-click any folder → "Git Bash Here"
# - Or search "Git Bash" in Start menu

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# LIMITATIONS OF GIT BASH
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ✅ Works:    bash syntax, grep, sed, awk, ssh, git
# ❌ Missing:  apt, systemctl, docker (native), many Linux tools
# ⚠️ Limited:  Process management, services
Option 3: PowerShell Equivalents Windows-native alternative commands
📋 Bash to PowerShell Quick Reference 
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              BASH → POWERSHELL COMMAND MAPPING                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│   BASH COMMAND          │ POWERSHELL EQUIVALENT                    │
│   ──────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── │
│   ls                    │ Get-ChildItem (or ls, dir)               │
│   ls -la                │ Get-ChildItem -Force                     │
│   cd                    │ Set-Location (or cd)                     │
│   pwd                   │ Get-Location (or pwd)                    │
│   cat file              │ Get-Content file (or cat, type)          │
│   head -n 10            │ Get-Content -Head 10                     │
│   tail -n 10            │ Get-Content -Tail 10                     │
│   tail -f               │ Get-Content -Wait -Tail 10               │
│   cp                    │ Copy-Item (or cp, copy)                  │
│   mv                    │ Move-Item (or mv, move)                  │
│   rm                    │ Remove-Item (or rm, del)                 │
│   rm -rf                │ Remove-Item -Recurse -Force              │
│   mkdir -p              │ New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force      │
│   touch file            │ New-Item file -ItemType File             │
│   grep pattern          │ Select-String -Pattern pattern           │
│   grep -r pattern       │ Get-ChildItem -Recurse │ Select-String  │
│   find . -name "*.txt"  │ Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter *.txt     │
│   echo $VAR             │ Write-Output $env:VAR (or echo)          │
│   export VAR=value      │ $env:VAR = "value"                       │
│   source file           │ . .\file.ps1                             │
│   chmod +x              │ (Not needed - use .ps1 extension)        │
│   which command         │ Get-Command command                      │
│   man command           │ Get-Help command                         │
│   ps aux                │ Get-Process                              │
│   kill PID              │ Stop-Process -Id PID                     │
│   curl URL              │ Invoke-WebRequest URL (or curl)          │
│   wget URL              │ Invoke-WebRequest -OutFile file URL      │
│   ssh user@host         │ ssh user@host (same in PowerShell 7+)    │
│   wc -l                 │ (Get-Content file).Count                 │
│   sort                  │ Sort-Object                              │
│   uniq                  │ Get-Unique (or Sort-Object -Unique)      │
│   xargs                 │ ForEach-Object                           │
│   &&                    │ ; or -and                                │
│   ||                    │ ; or -or                                 │
│   |                     │ | (same)                                 │
│   >                     │ > or Out-File                            │
│   >>                    │ >> or Out-File -Append                   │
│   2>&1                  │ *>&1                                     │
│   $?                    │ $LASTEXITCODE                            │
│   $$                    │ $PID                                     │
│                                                                     │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ PowerShell Script Basics
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# POWERSHELL SCRIPT STRUCTURE (.ps1 file)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Enable strict mode (like bash set -euo pipefail)
Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
$ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"

# Variables
$Name = "World"
$Count = 10

# String interpolation
Write-Host "Hello, $Name!"

# Arrays
$Servers = @("web1", "web2", "db1")
foreach ($Server in $Servers) {
    Write-Host "Processing $Server"
}

# Hashtables (like bash associative arrays)
$Config = @{
    Host = "localhost"
    Port = 8000
    Debug = $true
}
Write-Host "Host: $($Config.Host)"

# Functions
function Deploy-App {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory=$true)]
        [string]$Environment,
        
        [string]$Version = "latest"
    )
    
    Write-Host "Deploying $Version to $Environment"
}

Deploy-App -Environment "production" -Version "2.1.0"

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# RUN POWERSHELL SCRIPTS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# First time: Allow script execution
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

# Run script
.\deploy.ps1
.\deploy.ps1 -Environment production
📝 Note About This Documentation

The examples throughout use Bash/Linux because:

  • Production servers run Linux (AWS, Azure, GCP)
  • Docker containers use Linux
  • CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, Jenkins) use Linux
  • Kubernetes nodes run Linux
  • Industry standard for DevOps

Recommendation: Use WSL 2 to practice these examples, then apply concepts in PowerShell when needed for Windows-specific tasks.

📋 Part 1: Foundations

Build a solid foundation in scripting and automation concepts. This part covers the fundamentals of scripting, command line operations, and version control best practices for automation code.

Chapter 1: Introduction to Scripting & Automation

Understanding the automation landscape for DevOps engineers

Chapter Topics

1.1 What is Scripting vs Programming vs Automation?
1.2 The Automation Pyramid: Scripts → Tools → Platforms
1.3 Why Scripting Matters for DevOps/Cloud Engineers
1.4 Types of Automation: Infrastructure, Configuration, Deployment, Operational
1.5 Choosing the Right Tool for the Task
1.6 Setting Up Your Automation Environment
1.7 The DevOps Automation Mindset
1.8 Cost-Benefit Analysis of Automation

1.1 What is Scripting vs Programming vs Automation?

Fundamentals Critical Beginner

Understanding the distinctions between scripting, programming, and automation in DevOps context

Comprehensive Overview

Scripting involves writing small, task-focused programs (scripts) that automate specific operations, typically interpreted at runtime. Scripts are usually single-file or few-file solutions designed to solve immediate problems without complex architecture.

Programming encompasses building larger, structured applications with proper architecture, design patterns, multiple modules, testing frameworks, and long-term maintainability in mind. Programs often compile to executables or large application bundles.

Automation is the broader practice of using technology (including scripts and programs) to perform tasks without human intervention. It's the goal, while scripting and programming are the means to achieve it.

Aspect Scripting Programming Automation
Purpose Quick task execution Build complex systems Eliminate manual work
Size 10-500 lines typically 1000+ lines, multiple files Varies (pipelines, workflows)
Execution Interpreted (runtime) Often compiled Scheduled/triggered
Languages Bash, Python, PowerShell Python (Django/FastAPI), TypeScript YAML, HCL, DSLs
Development Time Minutes to hours Days to months Hours to weeks
🔧 Scripting

Quick solutions, single files, immediate results. "I need this done NOW"

deploy.sh → runs → server updated
backup.py → runs → data exported
💻 Programming

Architected applications, reusable, maintainable. "The team needs this for years"

django-app/
├── models.py
├── views.py
└── tests/
⚡ Automation

Orchestrated workflows, triggered actions. "This happens without me"

git push → CI/CD → tests → deploy
schedule → backup → notify
Example 1: Simple Bash Script (Scripting Approach) Scenario: Deploy a Django application to a single server - quick and dirty approach
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
anihpj/
├── manage.py
├── requirements.txt
├── anihpj/
│   ├── settings.py
│   └── wsgi.py
├── scripts/
│   └── deploy.sh          ← Our scripting solution
└── apps/
    └── core/
🐚 scripts/deploy.sh Scripting 
#!/bin/bash
#============================================================
# deploy.sh - Simple Deployment Script (SCRIPTING Approach)
#============================================================
# WHAT THIS IS: A SCRIPT - single file, immediate purpose, interpreted
# 
# WHY SCRIPTING HERE?
# • Quick to write (took 10 minutes)
# • Single purpose (deploy Django app)
# • No complex architecture needed
# • Runs once and done
#
# WHEN TO USE THIS APPROACH:
# • You need something working NOW
# • Task is straightforward
# • You're the only one running it
# • It's a one-off or infrequent task
#============================================================

set -e  # Exit immediately if any command fails (CRITICAL for deployments!)
        # Without this, script continues even after errors = broken deployment

echo "🚀 Starting deployment..."

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Get Latest Code
# Why: Ensure we're deploying the most recent version
#------------------------------------------------------------
cd /var/www/anihpj          # Navigate to project directory
git pull origin main         # Fetch and merge latest changes from main branch

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Update Python Dependencies
# Why: New code might require new packages
#------------------------------------------------------------
source venv/bin/activate     # Activate virtual environment (isolates dependencies)
pip install -r requirements.txt  # Install/update all packages listed in requirements

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Database Migrations
# Why: New code might have database schema changes
#------------------------------------------------------------
python manage.py migrate     # Apply any pending database migrations
                            # Django tracks which migrations have run

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 4: Static Files
# Why: CSS, JS, images need to be collected for production
#------------------------------------------------------------
python manage.py collectstatic --noinput  # Gather all static files
                                          # --noinput = don't ask for confirmation

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 5: Restart Services
# Why: Running processes need to reload the new code
#------------------------------------------------------------
sudo systemctl restart gunicorn  # Restart the Python WSGI server
sudo systemctl restart nginx     # Restart the web server (clears cache)

echo "✅ Deployment complete!"

# TOTAL TIME: ~2 minutes to run
# TOTAL LINES: ~30 (this is a SCRIPT, not a program)
▶️ Execute Script
# Make executable and run
chmod +x scripts/deploy.sh
./scripts/deploy.sh
📤 CLI Output
🚀 Starting deployment...
Already up to date.
Requirement already satisfied: Django==4.2 in ./venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages
...
Running migrations:
  Applying core.0015_auto... OK
128 static files copied to '/var/www/anihpj/staticfiles'.
✅ Deployment complete!
✔️ Verification
# Check services are running
systemctl status gunicorn | head -5
curl -I http://localhost:8000/health/
Example 2: Python Deployment Tool (Programming Approach) Scenario: Build a reusable, configurable deployment tool with proper architecture
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
deploy-tool/
├── setup.py
├── pyproject.toml
├── requirements.txt
├── tests/
│   ├── test_deployer.py
│   └── test_config.py
├── deploy_tool/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── main.py             ← Entry point
│   ├── deployer.py         ← Core logic
│   ├── config.py           ← Configuration handling
│   ├── services/
│   │   ├── git_service.py
│   │   ├── docker_service.py
│   │   └── k8s_service.py
│   └── utils/
│       ├── logger.py
│       └── validators.py
└── configs/
    ├── production.yaml
    └── staging.yaml
🐍 deploy_tool/deployer.py Programming 
"""
============================================================
Deployer Module - Core Deployment Logic (PROGRAMMING Approach)
============================================================
WHAT THIS IS: A PROGRAM - multiple files, classes, design patterns, testable

WHY PROGRAMMING HERE (instead of a simple script)?
• Used by 50+ developers daily
• Needs to support multiple deployment strategies (Rolling, Blue-Green, Canary)
• Requires proper error handling, logging, and audit trails
• Must be testable (unit tests, integration tests)
• Will be maintained and extended for years

CONCEPTS DEMONSTRATED:
• Strategy Pattern (swappable deployment methods)
• Dependency Injection (testable, loosely coupled)
• Type Hints (better IDE support, documentation)
• Dataclasses (clean data structures)
============================================================
"""
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod      # For creating abstract base classes
from dataclasses import dataclass        # Python 3.7+ for clean data structures
from typing import List, Optional        # Type hints for better code quality
import logging                           # Standard logging (not print statements!)

from .config import DeployConfig         # Configuration from separate module
from .services.git_service import GitService      # Git operations abstracted
from .services.docker_service import DockerService # Docker operations abstracted

# Create a logger named after this module (best practice for tracing)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass  # This decorator auto-generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__ methods
class DeploymentResult:
    """
    Immutable result object for deployment operations.
    
    WHY USE A DATACLASS?
    • Cleaner than dictionaries (IDE autocomplete, type checking)
    • Automatic __init__ and __repr__
    • Can be easily serialized to JSON for APIs
    • Self-documenting with type hints
    
    EXAMPLE USAGE:
        result = DeploymentResult(success=True, environment="prod", ...)
        if result.success:
            print(f"Deployed {result.version} in {result.duration_seconds}s")
    """
    success: bool              # Did the deployment succeed?
    environment: str           # Which environment (staging, production)?
    version: str              # What version was deployed (e.g., "1.2.3")?
    duration_seconds: float    # How long did it take?
    errors: List[str]         # List of any errors that occurred


class DeploymentStrategy(ABC):
    """
    Abstract base class for deployment strategies.
    
    THIS IS THE STRATEGY PATTERN:
    • Define a family of algorithms (deployment methods)
    • Make them interchangeable
    • Client code doesn't need to know which strategy is used
    
    WHY USE THIS PATTERN?
    • Different teams might need different deployment approaches
    • Easy to add new strategies without changing existing code
    • Each strategy can be tested in isolation
    
    SUBCLASSES MUST IMPLEMENT: deploy()
    """
    
    @abstractmethod  # Forces subclasses to implement this method
    def deploy(self, config: DeployConfig) -> DeploymentResult:
        """Execute deployment according to this strategy's algorithm."""
        pass  # No implementation here - subclasses provide it


class RollingDeployment(DeploymentStrategy):
    """
    Rolling Deployment Strategy - Zero Downtime Deployments
    
    HOW ROLLING DEPLOYMENT WORKS:
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │ Pod 1: v1.0 ──► v1.1 (updated first)               │
    │ Pod 2: v1.0 (still serving traffic)                │
    │ Pod 3: v1.0 (still serving traffic)                │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    Then Pod 2, then Pod 3... gradually rolling through
    
    ADVANTAGES:
    • Zero downtime (some pods always running)
    • Easy rollback (stop and some pods still on old version)
    • Gradual traffic shift (can detect issues early)
    
    USE WHEN: Most production deployments
    """
    
    def __init__(self, git: GitService, docker: DockerService):
        """
        Initialize with required services.
        
        DEPENDENCY INJECTION:
        Services are passed IN, not created here. This allows:
        • Unit testing with mock services
        • Swapping implementations without changing this class
        • Loose coupling between components
        """
        self.git = git
        self.docker = docker
    
    def deploy(self, config: DeployConfig) -> DeploymentResult:
        """
        Execute a rolling deployment.
        
        Args:
            config: DeployConfig with environment, version, branch, etc.
            
        Returns:
            DeploymentResult with success status and metadata
            
        Raises:
            GitError: If checkout fails
            DockerError: If build or push fails
        """
        logger.info(f"Starting rolling deployment to {config.environment}")
        
        # Each step is clear, logged, and can fail independently
        self.git.checkout(config.branch)    # Step 1: Get the code
        image_tag = self.docker.build(      # Step 2: Build container image
            config.dockerfile, 
            config.version
        )
        self.docker.push(image_tag)         # Step 3: Push to registry
        
        # Return structured result (much better than True/False)
        return DeploymentResult(
            success=True,
            environment=config.environment,
            version=config.version,
            duration_seconds=45.2,
            errors=[]
        )


class Deployer:
    """
    Main Deployer Class - Orchestrates the Deployment Process
    
    THIS IS THE CONTEXT IN STRATEGY PATTERN:
    • Holds a reference to the current strategy
    • Delegates the actual work to the strategy
    • Can switch strategies at runtime if needed
    
    SEPARATION OF CONCERNS:
    • Deployer: orchestration, logging, pre/post hooks
    • Strategy: actual deployment algorithm
    """
    
    def __init__(self, strategy: DeploymentStrategy):
        """Store the deployment strategy to use."""
        self._strategy = strategy  # Could be Rolling, BlueGreen, Canary, etc.
    
    def execute(self, config: DeployConfig) -> DeploymentResult:
        """Execute deployment using the configured strategy."""
        return self._strategy.deploy(config)
🐍 deploy_tool/main.py 
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Deploy Tool - Enterprise-grade deployment automation
Usage: deploy-tool --env production --version 1.2.3
"""
import argparse
import sys
from pathlib import Path

from .config import load_config
from .deployer import Deployer, RollingDeployment
from .services.git_service import GitService
from .services.docker_service import DockerService


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Deploy applications')
    parser.add_argument('--env', required=True, choices=['staging', 'production'])
    parser.add_argument('--version', required=True)
    parser.add_argument('--dry-run', action='store_true')
    args = parser.parse_args()
    
    config = load_config(args.env)
    config.version = args.version
    
    strategy = RollingDeployment(GitService(), DockerService())
    deployer = Deployer(strategy)
    
    result = deployer.execute(config)
    
    if result.success:
        print(f"✅ Deployed {result.version} to {result.environment}")
        sys.exit(0)
    else:
        print(f"❌ Deployment failed: {result.errors}")
        sys.exit(1)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
▶️ Execute Program
# Install the tool
pip install -e .

# Run with arguments
deploy-tool --env production --version 1.2.3
📤 CLI Output
2024-01-15 10:30:22 INFO Starting rolling deployment to production
2024-01-15 10:30:23 INFO Checking out branch: main
2024-01-15 10:30:45 INFO Building Docker image: anihpj:1.2.3
2024-01-15 10:31:02 INFO Pushing image to registry
2024-01-15 10:31:15 INFO Updating Kubernetes deployment
✅ Deployed 1.2.3 to production
Example 3: GitHub Actions Workflow (Automation Approach) Scenario: Fully automated deployment triggered by git push - no human intervention
📁 Project Context Files
🐳 Dockerfile 
# Multi-stage build for Django application
FROM python:3.11-slim as builder

WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip wheel --no-cache-dir --no-deps --wheel-dir /app/wheels -r requirements.txt

FROM python:3.11-slim

ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=builder /app/wheels /wheels
RUN pip install --no-cache /wheels/*

COPY . .
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
☸️ k8s/deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:${CI_COMMIT_TAG}
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: database-url
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "200m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
⚙️ .github/workflows/deploy.yml Automation 
# ============================================================
# GitHub Actions Workflow - AUTOMATION approach
# ============================================================
# This is AUTOMATION: triggers automatically on git events
# No human intervention needed - push code, it deploys!
# ============================================================

name: Deploy Pipeline

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │ TRIGGERS: When does this workflow run?                  │
# │ • push to main → test + build + deploy staging          │
# │ • push tags v* → deploy to production                   │
# │ • pull requests → run tests only                        │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ['v*']
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  DOCKER_IMAGE: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io

jobs:
  # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 1: TEST - Runs on every push and PR
  # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
          cache: 'pip'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      
      - name: Run tests with coverage
        run: |
          coverage run manage.py test
          coverage report --fail-under=80

  # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 2: BUILD - Build and push Docker image
  # Only runs on main branch pushes (not PRs)
  # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  build:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'push'
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Login to GitHub Container Registry
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      
      - name: Build and push Docker image
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          push: true
          tags: |
            ${{ env.DOCKER_IMAGE }}:${{ github.sha }}
            ${{ env.DOCKER_IMAGE }}:latest

  # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 3: DEPLOY STAGING - Auto-deploy on main pushes
  # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  deploy-staging:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    environment:
      name: staging
      url: https://staging.anihpj.com
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes (Staging)
        run: |
          echo "Deploying to staging..."
          kubectl config use-context staging
          envsubst < k8s/deployment.yaml | kubectl apply -f -
          kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-web -n staging

  # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 4: DEPLOY PRODUCTION - Manual approval required
  # Only runs on version tags (v1.0.0, v1.2.3, etc.)
  # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  deploy-production:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://anihpj.com
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes (Production)
        run: |
          echo "Deploying ${{ github.ref_name }} to production..."
          kubectl config use-context production
          export IMAGE_TAG=${{ github.ref_name }}
          envsubst < k8s/deployment.yaml | kubectl apply -f -
          kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-web -n production
▶️ Trigger Automation
# Automation is triggered by git operations - no manual intervention!

# Push to main → triggers test + build + staging deploy
git push origin main

# Create release tag → triggers production deploy
git tag -a v1.2.3 -m "Release 1.2.3"
git push origin v1.2.3
📤 Workflow Output (GitHub Actions UI)
Pipeline #12345 for v1.2.3
├── ✅ test ................ passed (2m 15s)
├── ✅ build ............... passed (4m 32s)
├── ✅ deploy-staging ...... passed (1m 45s)
└── ⏸️ deploy-production ... waiting for manual action

Staging Environment: https://staging.anihpj.com ✅ Healthy
Production Environment: Ready to deploy v1.2.3
Example 4: Same Task - Three Approaches Compared Scenario: Backup a PostgreSQL database - showing script vs program vs automation
📁 Approach 1: Scripting (Quick Backup)
🐚 backup.sh Script 
#!/bin/bash
# Quick backup script - runs manually when needed
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
BACKUP_FILE="/backups/anihpj_${DATE}.sql.gz"

pg_dump $DATABASE_URL | gzip > $BACKUP_FILE
aws s3 cp $BACKUP_FILE s3://anihpj-backups/

echo "Backup complete: $BACKUP_FILE"
📁 Approach 2: Programming (Backup Tool)
🐍 backup_tool/backup.py Program 
"""
Enterprise backup tool with retention, encryption, and verification
"""
import subprocess
import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from cryptography.fernet import Fernet
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import logging

@dataclass
class BackupConfig:
    database_url: str
    s3_bucket: str
    retention_days: int = 30
    encrypt: bool = True
    
class BackupManager:
    def __init__(self, config: BackupConfig):
        self.config = config
        self.s3 = boto3.client('s3')
        self.logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
    
    def create_backup(self) -> str:
        """Create, compress, encrypt, and upload backup"""
        timestamp = datetime.now().strftime('%Y%m%d_%H%M%S')
        filename = f"anihpj_{timestamp}.sql.gz.enc"
        
        # Dump → Compress → Encrypt pipeline
        dump = subprocess.run(
            ['pg_dump', self.config.database_url],
            capture_output=True, check=True
        )
        
        compressed = self._compress(dump.stdout)
        encrypted = self._encrypt(compressed) if self.config.encrypt else compressed
        
        self.s3.put_object(
            Bucket=self.config.s3_bucket,
            Key=f"backups/{filename}",
            Body=encrypted
        )
        
        self.logger.info(f"Backup created: {filename}")
        return filename
    
    def cleanup_old_backups(self):
        """Remove backups older than retention period"""
        cutoff = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=self.config.retention_days)
        # ... cleanup logic
    
    def verify_backup(self, filename: str) -> bool:
        """Verify backup integrity"""
        # ... verification logic
        return True
📁 Approach 3: Automation (Scheduled + Self-Healing)
☸️ k8s/backup-cronjob.yaml Automation 
# Kubernetes CronJob - fully automated backups
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: postgres-backup
  namespace: production
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"  # Daily at 2 AM
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: backup-sa
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: registry.example.com/backup-tool:latest
            command:
            - python
            - -m
            - backup_tool
            - --action=backup
            - --verify
            - --cleanup
            env:
            - name: DATABASE_URL
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-credentials
                  key: url
            - name: S3_BUCKET
              value: anihpj-backups
            - name: RETENTION_DAYS
              value: "30"
            resources:
              requests:
                memory: "256Mi"
                cpu: "100m"
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
# Alert if backup fails
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: backup-alerts
spec:
  groups:
  - name: backup
    rules:
    - alert: BackupFailed
      expr: kube_job_failed{job_name=~"postgres-backup.*"} > 0
      for: 5m
      labels:
        severity: critical
      annotations:
        summary: "Database backup failed!"
▶️ Comparison of Execution
# SCRIPTING: Manual execution
./backup.sh

# PROGRAMMING: CLI execution with options
backup-tool --action backup --verify --encrypt

# AUTOMATION: Zero-touch - runs automatically every day at 2 AM
kubectl get cronjobs -n production
kubectl get jobs -n production | grep postgres-backup
📤 When to Use Each
┌─────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ SCRIPTING       │ One-off tasks, quick fixes, prototyping              │
│                 │ "I need to backup NOW and don't care about features" │
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ PROGRAMMING     │ Reusable tools, complex logic, team collaboration    │
│                 │ "We need a backup tool used across 10 projects"      │
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ AUTOMATION      │ Recurring tasks, compliance, reliability             │
│                 │ "Backups must happen daily without anyone doing it"  │
└─────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Example 5: DevOps Decision Matrix - What to Use When Scenario: Choosing the right approach for common DevOps tasks
📁 Decision Framework
📋 devops-decision-matrix.md 
# DevOps Approach Decision Matrix

## Quick Decision Tree

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Is this a one-time task?                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                        │
           ┌────────────┴────────────┐
           │                         │
          YES                        NO
           │                         │
           ▼                         ▼
    ┌──────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────┐
    │   SCRIPT IT  │    │ Will it run on schedule │
    │  (Bash/Py)   │    │    or be triggered?     │
    └──────────────┘    └─────────────────────────┘
                                    │
                       ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                       │                         │
                      YES                        NO
                       │                         │
                       ▼                         ▼
              ┌──────────────┐        ┌──────────────────┐
              │  AUTOMATE IT │        │ Does it need to  │
              │  (CI/CD/K8s) │        │ be shared/reused │
              └──────────────┘        │ across projects? │
                                      └──────────────────┘
                                                │
                                   ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                                   │                         │
                                  YES                        NO
                                   │                         │
                                   ▼                         ▼
                          ┌──────────────┐          ┌──────────────┐
                          │  PROGRAM IT  │          │   SCRIPT IT  │
                          │  (Full tool) │          │   (Simple)   │
                          └──────────────┘          └──────────────┘

## Common DevOps Tasks by Approach

| Task                          | Scripting | Programming | Automation |
|-------------------------------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| Quick log analysis            | ✅        |             |            |
| Server provisioning (once)    | ✅        |             |            |
| Custom CLI tool for team      |           | ✅          |            |
| Internal deployment framework |           | ✅          |            |
| CI/CD pipeline                |           |             | ✅         |
| Database backups              |           |             | ✅         |
| Certificate renewal           |           |             | ✅         |
| Security scanning             |           |             | ✅         |
| Log rotation                  |           |             | ✅         |
| Health check monitoring       |           |             | ✅         |
⚡ real-world-examples.yaml 
# Real-world examples from production environments

scripting_examples:
  - name: "Emergency disk cleanup"
    description: "Server running out of space, need to clean NOW"
    tool: "Bash one-liner"
    example: "find /var/log -name '*.log' -mtime +7 -delete"
    
  - name: "Quick data export"
    description: "CEO needs a CSV report in 10 minutes"
    tool: "Python script"
    example: "python export_users.py > users.csv"

programming_examples:
  - name: "Internal deployment CLI"
    description: "Used by 50 developers daily to deploy services"
    tool: "Python (Click + Rich)"
    features:
      - Multiple deployment strategies
      - Rollback support
      - Audit logging
      - Slack notifications
      
  - name: "Cost analysis tool"
    description: "Weekly AWS cost reports with recommendations"
    tool: "Python (boto3 + pandas)"
    features:
      - Multi-account support
      - Historical trending
      - Email reports

automation_examples:
  - name: "CI/CD Pipeline"
    description: "Every git push triggers automated workflow"
    tool: "GitHub Actions"
    triggers:
      - push_to_main
      - pull_request
      - scheduled (nightly)
      
  - name: "Infrastructure as Code"
    description: "Git push to infra repo provisions resources"
    tool: "Terraform + Atlantis"
    workflow:
      - PR opened → terraform plan
      - PR approved → terraform apply
      
  - name: "Auto-scaling"
    description: "Kubernetes automatically scales pods"
    tool: "HPA / KEDA"
    triggers:
      - CPU > 70%
      - Queue depth > 100
💡 Key Takeaways
1. SCRIPTING is for speed and simplicity
   → Use when: Quick one-off task, prototyping, personal use
   → Time investment: Minutes to hours
   
2. PROGRAMMING is for scale and maintainability  
   → Use when: Team tool, complex logic, needs testing
   → Time investment: Days to weeks
   
3. AUTOMATION is for reliability and consistency
   → Use when: Recurring task, compliance requirement, self-healing
   → Time investment: Hours to days (but saves months long-term)

As a DevOps engineer, you'll use ALL THREE daily:
- Morning: Write a script to analyze yesterday's errors
- Afternoon: Add a feature to your team's deployment tool
- Evening: Set up automated certificate renewal

Master all three - they complement each other!

1.2 The Automation Pyramid: Scripts → Tools → Platforms

Architecture High Beginner

Understanding the hierarchy of automation from ad-hoc scripts to enterprise platforms

Comprehensive Overview

The Automation Pyramid is a conceptual framework that describes how automation evolves in organizations - from simple scripts at the base to sophisticated platforms at the top. Understanding this hierarchy helps DevOps engineers choose the right level of abstraction for each task.

                    ┌─────────────────┐
                    │    PLATFORMS    │  ← Kubernetes, Terraform Cloud
                    │   (Self-serve)  │     Backstage, Humanitec
                    ├─────────────────┤
                    │     TOOLS &     │  ← Ansible, Terraform, Helm
                    │   FRAMEWORKS    │     Docker, ArgoCD
                    ├─────────────────┤
                    │    LIBRARIES    │  ← boto3, kubernetes-client
                    │   & SDK/APIs    │     fabric, paramiko
                    ├─────────────────┤
                    │     SCRIPTS     │  ← Bash, Python, PowerShell
                    │   (Ad-hoc)      │     One-off automation
                    └─────────────────┘
                    
        COMPLEXITY ▲                    ▲ ABSTRACTION
        TIME TO BUILD                     REUSABILITY
        MAINTENANCE                       STANDARDIZATION
Level Examples When to Use Investment
Scripts Bash, Python one-offs Quick fixes, prototyping Minutes-Hours
Libraries/SDKs boto3, kubernetes-client Programmatic cloud access Hours-Days
Tools Terraform, Ansible, Helm Repeatable infrastructure Days-Weeks
Platforms Kubernetes, Backstage Enterprise standardization Weeks-Months
Example 1: Level 1 - Scripts (Base of Pyramid) Scenario: Quick script to restart all unhealthy pods in Kubernetes
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
anihpj/
├── scripts/
│   └── ops/
│       ├── restart-unhealthy.sh    ← Level 1: Ad-hoc script
│       └── cleanup-pods.sh
└── k8s/
    └── deployment.yaml
☸️ k8s/deployment.yaml 
# Current deployment - some pods might be unhealthy
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:1.2.3
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 3
🐚 scripts/ops/restart-unhealthy.sh Level 1: Script 
#!/bin/bash
#============================================================
# restart-unhealthy.sh - Quick Script to Restart Unhealthy Pods
#============================================================
# PYRAMID LEVEL 1: Ad-hoc script for immediate problem solving
#
# SITUATION: It's 3 AM, pods are crashing, you need to fix it NOW
# This script finds and restarts unhealthy pods automatically.
#
# WHY A SCRIPT (not a tool)?
# • Need it working in 5 minutes
# • One-time or rare use
# • Direct kubectl commands are the right tool
# • No complex logic needed
#
# USAGE: ./restart-unhealthy.sh [namespace]
#============================================================

# PARAMETER: Namespace to check (defaults to 'production' if not provided)
# The ${1:-production} syntax means: use $1 if provided, else use 'production'
NAMESPACE=${1:-production}

echo "🔍 Finding unhealthy pods in $NAMESPACE..."

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Find pods that aren't in "Running" state
#------------------------------------------------------------
# kubectl get pods: List all pods
# -o jsonpath: Output only specific fields (not full YAML)
# The JSONPath query finds pods where status.phase != "Running"
# Possible phases: Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, Unknown
UNHEALTHY_PODS=$(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE \
    -o jsonpath='{.items[?(@.status.phase!="Running")].metadata.name}')

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Also find pods that are Running but NOT Ready
#------------------------------------------------------------
# A pod can be "Running" but still unhealthy if containers aren't ready
# -o json: Full JSON output (needed for complex filtering)
# jq: JSON processor (filters pods where ready==false)
# This catches pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff, ImagePullBackOff, etc.
UNHEALTHY_PODS="$UNHEALTHY_PODS $(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE \
    -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.status.containerStatuses[]?.ready==false) | .metadata.name')"

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Deduplicate the list
#------------------------------------------------------------
# tr ' ' '\n': Convert spaces to newlines (one pod per line)
# sort -u: Sort and remove duplicates (-u = unique)
UNHEALTHY_PODS=$(echo $UNHEALTHY_PODS | tr ' ' '\n' | sort -u)

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 4: Exit early if everything is healthy
#------------------------------------------------------------
# -z: Test if string is empty
if [ -z "$UNHEALTHY_PODS" ]; then
    echo "✅ All pods are healthy!"
    exit 0  # Exit with success code
fi

# Show what we found (always good to log before taking action!)
echo "⚠️  Found unhealthy pods:"
echo "$UNHEALTHY_PODS"

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 5: Delete each unhealthy pod
#------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY DELETE? Kubernetes Deployment will automatically recreate them
# This is the "turn it off and on again" of Kubernetes
for POD in $UNHEALTHY_PODS; do
    echo "🔄 Restarting: $POD"
    kubectl delete pod $POD -n $NAMESPACE
    # Kubernetes sees the pod count is below desired replicas
    # and immediately schedules a new pod to replace it
done

#------------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 6: Wait for rollout to complete
#------------------------------------------------------------
echo "✅ Restart initiated. Monitoring..."
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-web -n $NAMESPACE
# This command blocks until deployment is healthy or times out
▶️ Execute Script
# Run the ad-hoc script
chmod +x scripts/ops/restart-unhealthy.sh
./scripts/ops/restart-unhealthy.sh production
📤 CLI Output
🔍 Finding unhealthy pods in production...
⚠️  Found unhealthy pods:
anihpj-web-7d8f9b7c4d-2xkjl
anihpj-web-7d8f9b7c4d-9mn3k
🔄 Restarting: anihpj-web-7d8f9b7c4d-2xkjl
pod "anihpj-web-7d8f9b7c4d-2xkjl" deleted
🔄 Restarting: anihpj-web-7d8f9b7c4d-9mn3k
pod "anihpj-web-7d8f9b7c4d-9mn3k" deleted
✅ Restart initiated. Monitoring...
Waiting for deployment "anihpj-web" rollout to finish...
deployment "anihpj-web" successfully rolled out
Example 2: Level 2 - Libraries/SDKs Scenario: Using kubernetes-client library for programmatic pod management
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
anihpj/
├── automation/
│   ├── requirements.txt
│   └── pod_manager.py          ← Level 2: SDK-based solution
└── k8s/
    └── deployment.yaml
📦 automation/requirements.txt 
kubernetes==28.1.0
rich==13.7.0
click==8.1.7
🐍 automation/pod_manager.py Level 2: SDK 
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
============================================================
Pod Manager - Using Kubernetes Python SDK
============================================================
PYRAMID LEVEL 2: Direct SDK/library usage for programmatic control

WHY LEVEL 2 (SDK) INSTEAD OF LEVEL 1 (SCRIPT)?
• Need structured data (not just text output)
• Want proper error handling (not just exit codes)
• Building a reusable tool (not one-off)
• Need rich output formatting (tables, colors)
• Integrating with other Python code

LIBRARIES USED:
• kubernetes: Official K8s Python client (talks to API server)
• rich: Beautiful terminal output (tables, colors, progress)
• click: CLI framework (arguments, help text, validation)

WHEN TO USE THIS LEVEL:
• Task is recurring (daily/weekly)
• Need to build on top of it (import as module)
• Require structured error handling
• Want nice CLI with help text
============================================================
"""
from kubernetes import client, config           # Official K8s SDK
from kubernetes.client.rest import ApiException # K8s-specific exceptions
from rich.console import Console                # Pretty terminal output
from rich.table import Table                    # ASCII tables
from dataclasses import dataclass               # Clean data structures
from typing import List                         # Type hints
import click                                    # CLI framework

# Rich console for pretty output (instead of plain print())
console = Console()


@dataclass  # Auto-generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__
class PodHealth:
    """
    Data class to hold pod health information.
    
    WHY A DATACLASS?
    • Clean, typed structure (better than dictionaries)
    • IDE autocomplete works
    • Easy to pass around between functions
    • Can add methods if needed later
    """
    name: str          # Pod name (e.g., "anihpj-web-7d8f9b7c4d-2xkjl")
    namespace: str     # Namespace (e.g., "production")
    phase: str         # Current phase (Running, Pending, Failed, etc.)
    ready: bool        # Are all containers ready?
    restarts: int      # Total container restarts (high = problem!)


class PodManager:
    """
    Kubernetes pod management using official Python client.
    
    THIS CLASS PROVIDES:
    • list_pods(): Get all pods with health status
    • get_unhealthy_pods(): Filter to only unhealthy ones
    • restart_pod(): Delete a pod to trigger recreation
    • display_status(): Pretty table output
    
    PATTERN: Encapsulate K8s operations so callers don't need to know
    the details of the K8s API.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, namespace: str = "production"):
        """
        Initialize the pod manager with Kubernetes configuration.
        
        This automatically detects whether running:
        • Inside a cluster (in-cluster config from service account)
        • Outside a cluster (uses ~/.kube/config file)
        """
        # Try in-cluster config first (when running as a K8s pod)
        try:
            config.load_incluster_config()
        except config.ConfigException:
            # Fall back to local kubeconfig (~/.kube/config)
            config.load_kube_config()
        
        # CoreV1Api handles pods, services, configmaps, etc.
        self.v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
        self.namespace = namespace
    
    def list_pods(self, label_selector: str = None) -> List[PodHealth]:
        """
        List all pods with health status.
        
        Args:
            label_selector: K8s label selector (e.g., "app=anihpj")
                           If None, returns ALL pods in namespace
        
        Returns:
            List of PodHealth objects with status information
        """
        # Call K8s API to get pods
        pods = self.v1.list_namespaced_pod(
            namespace=self.namespace,
            label_selector=label_selector
        )
        
        results = []
        for pod in pods.items:
            # A pod is "ready" only if ALL containers are ready
            # Using all() with generator expression for efficiency
            ready = all(
                cs.ready for cs in (pod.status.container_statuses or [])
            )
            # Total restarts across all containers
            # High restart count = something is crashing
            restarts = sum(
                cs.restart_count for cs in (pod.status.container_statuses or [])
            )
            results.append(PodHealth(
                name=pod.metadata.name,
                namespace=pod.metadata.namespace,
                phase=pod.status.phase,
                ready=ready,
                restarts=restarts
            ))
        return results
    
    def get_unhealthy_pods(self) -> List[PodHealth]:
        """
        Find pods that are not running or not ready.
        
        UNHEALTHY MEANS:
        • Phase is not "Running" (Pending, Failed, Unknown)
        • OR containers are not ready (CrashLoopBackOff, etc.)
        """
        all_pods = self.list_pods()
        # List comprehension to filter unhealthy pods
        return [p for p in all_pods if p.phase != "Running" or not p.ready]
    
    def restart_pod(self, pod_name: str) -> bool:
        """
        Delete a pod to trigger restart (K8s Deployment will recreate it).
        
        WHY DELETE?
        In Kubernetes, you don't "restart" pods directly.
        The Deployment controller sees replica count dropped and creates new pod.
        
        Returns:
            True if deletion succeeded, False otherwise
        """
        try:
            self.v1.delete_namespaced_pod(
                name=pod_name,
                namespace=self.namespace
            )
            console.print(f"[green]✓[/green] Deleted pod: {pod_name}")
            return True
        except ApiException as e:
            # ApiException contains K8s-specific error details
            console.print(f"[red]✗[/red] Failed to delete {pod_name}: {e.reason}")
            return False
    
    def display_status(self):
        """
        Display pod status in a beautiful Rich table.
        
        WHY RICH TABLES?
        • Much more readable than plain text
        • Colors highlight problems
        • Professional-looking output
        """
        pods = self.list_pods(label_selector="app=anihpj")
        
        # Create Rich table with columns
        table = Table(title=f"Pod Status - {self.namespace}")
        table.add_column("Pod Name", style="cyan")
        table.add_column("Phase", style="magenta")
        table.add_column("Ready", justify="center")
        table.add_column("Restarts", justify="right")
        
        for pod in pods:
            ready_icon = "✅" if pod.ready else "❌"
            phase_color = "green" if pod.phase == "Running" else "red"
            table.add_row(
                pod.name,
                f"[{phase_color}]{pod.phase}[/{phase_color}]",
                ready_icon,
                str(pod.restarts)
            )
        
        console.print(table)


# ============================================================
# CLI INTERFACE using Click
# ============================================================
# Click decorators turn this function into a full CLI with:
# • --help automatically generated
# • Argument parsing and validation
# • Type conversion

@click.command()
@click.option('--namespace', '-n', default='production', 
              help='Kubernetes namespace to manage')
@click.option('--restart-unhealthy', is_flag=True, 
              help='Automatically restart unhealthy pods')
def main(namespace: str, restart_unhealthy: bool):
    """
    Pod Manager CLI - Kubernetes SDK Level 2 automation
    
    Examples:
        python pod_manager.py                    # Show status
        python pod_manager.py -n staging         # Different namespace  
        python pod_manager.py --restart-unhealthy  # Auto-fix
    """
    manager = PodManager(namespace)
    
    # Always show current status
    manager.display_status()
    
    # Optionally restart unhealthy pods
    if restart_unhealthy:
        unhealthy = manager.get_unhealthy_pods()
        if unhealthy:
            console.print(f"\n[yellow]Found {len(unhealthy)} unhealthy pods[/yellow]")
            for pod in unhealthy:
                manager.restart_pod(pod.name)
        else:
            console.print("\n[green]All pods are healthy![/green]")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()  # Click handles argument parsing and calls main()
▶️ Execute SDK-based Tool
# Install dependencies and run
pip install -r automation/requirements.txt
python automation/pod_manager.py --namespace production --restart-unhealthy

Example 3: Level 3 - Tools & Frameworks (Terraform + Helm) Scenario: Declarative infrastructure and application deployment with proper tooling
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
anihpj/
├── terraform/                   ← Level 3: Infrastructure as Code
│   ├── main.tf
│   ├── variables.tf
│   ├── outputs.tf
│   └── environments/
│       ├── staging.tfvars
│       └── production.tfvars
├── helm/
│   └── anihpj/                  ← Level 3: Helm chart
│       ├── Chart.yaml
│       ├── values.yaml
│       ├── values-staging.yaml
│       ├── values-production.yaml
│       └── templates/
│           ├── deployment.yaml
│           ├── service.yaml
│           ├── ingress.yaml
│           └── hpa.yaml
└── src/
🏗️ terraform/main.tf Level 3: IaC Tool 
# Terraform - Level 3 of the Automation Pyramid
# Declarative infrastructure definition

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
    kubernetes = {
      source  = "hashicorp/kubernetes"
      version = "~> 2.23"
    }
    helm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/helm"
      version = "~> 2.11"
    }
  }
  
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "anihpj-terraform-state"
    key            = "infrastructure/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}

# EKS Cluster
module "eks" {
  source          = "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws"
  version         = "19.17.2"
  
  cluster_name    = "anihpj-${var.environment}"
  cluster_version = "1.28"
  
  vpc_id          = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids      = module.vpc.private_subnets
  
  eks_managed_node_groups = {
    main = {
      desired_size = var.node_count
      min_size     = var.node_count_min
      max_size     = var.node_count_max
      
      instance_types = var.instance_types
      capacity_type  = "ON_DEMAND"
    }
  }
}

# RDS PostgreSQL
module "rds" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/rds/aws"
  version = "6.3.0"
  
  identifier           = "anihpj-${var.environment}"
  engine               = "postgres"
  engine_version       = "15.4"
  family               = "postgres15"
  major_engine_version = "15"
  instance_class       = var.db_instance_class
  
  allocated_storage     = var.db_storage_gb
  max_allocated_storage = var.db_max_storage_gb
  
  db_name  = "anihpj"
  username = "anihpj_admin"
  port     = 5432
  
  vpc_security_group_ids = [module.security_group.security_group_id]
  db_subnet_group_name   = module.vpc.database_subnet_group_name
  
  backup_retention_period = var.environment == "production" ? 30 : 7
  deletion_protection     = var.environment == "production"
}

# Deploy Helm chart
resource "helm_release" "anihpj" {
  name       = "anihpj"
  namespace  = var.environment
  chart      = "../helm/anihpj"
  
  values = [
    file("../helm/anihpj/values-${var.environment}.yaml")
  ]
  
  set {
    name  = "image.tag"
    value = var.app_version
  }
  
  set_sensitive {
    name  = "database.url"
    value = "postgres://${module.rds.db_instance_username}:${random_password.db.result}@${module.rds.db_instance_endpoint}/${module.rds.db_instance_name}"
  }
  
  depends_on = [module.eks, module.rds]
}
⎈ helm/anihpj/Chart.yaml 
apiVersion: v2
name: anihpj
description: Django application Helm chart
type: application
version: 1.0.0
appVersion: "1.2.3"

dependencies:
  - name: redis
    version: "18.x.x"
    repository: "https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami"
    condition: redis.enabled
⎈ helm/anihpj/templates/deployment.yaml Level 3: Helm 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: {{ include "anihpj.fullname" . }}
  labels:
    {{- include "anihpj.labels" . | nindent 4 }}
spec:
  {{- if not .Values.autoscaling.enabled }}
  replicas: {{ .Values.replicaCount }}
  {{- end }}
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      {{- include "anihpj.selectorLabels" . | nindent 6 }}
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        checksum/config: {{ include (print $.Template.BasePath "/configmap.yaml") . | sha256sum }}
      labels:
        {{- include "anihpj.selectorLabels" . | nindent 8 }}
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: {{ .Chart.Name }}
          image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}"
          imagePullPolicy: {{ .Values.image.pullPolicy }}
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: {{ include "anihpj.fullname" . }}-config
            - secretRef:
                name: {{ include "anihpj.fullname" . }}-secrets
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health/
              port: http
            initialDelaySeconds: {{ .Values.probes.liveness.initialDelaySeconds }}
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready/
              port: http
            initialDelaySeconds: {{ .Values.probes.readiness.initialDelaySeconds }}
          resources:
            {{- toYaml .Values.resources | nindent 12 }}
▶️ Execute Level 3 Tools
# Terraform - Infrastructure provisioning
cd terraform
terraform init
terraform plan -var-file=environments/production.tfvars
terraform apply -var-file=environments/production.tfvars -auto-approve

# Helm - Application deployment (alternative to terraform helm_release)
helm upgrade --install anihpj ./helm/anihpj \
    --namespace production \
    --values ./helm/anihpj/values-production.yaml \
    --set image.tag=1.2.3

TEST SUITE: None
NOTES:

  Get pods: kubectl get pods -n production -l app.kubernetes.io/name=anihpj
Example 4: Level 4 - Platforms (Backstage Internal Developer Portal) Scenario: Self-service platform where developers deploy without knowing underlying tools
📁 Project Context Files
📄 platform-architecture 
platform-infrastructure/
├── backstage/                       ← Level 4: Developer Platform
│   ├── catalog-info.yaml
│   ├── templates/
│   │   ├── django-service/
│   │   │   └── template.yaml        ← Self-service template
│   │   └── microservice/
│   │       └── template.yaml
│   └── app-config.yaml
├── argocd/                          ← GitOps underneath
│   ├── applications/
│   │   └── anihpj.yaml
│   └── appprojects/
└── crossplane/                      ← Infrastructure abstraction
    └── compositions/
        └── database.yaml
🎭 backstage/templates/django-service/template.yaml Level 4: Platform 
# Backstage Software Template - Level 4 of Automation Pyramid
# Developers fill out a form, click "Create", everything is automated
apiVersion: scaffolder.backstage.io/v1beta3
kind: Template
metadata:
  name: django-service
  title: Django Service
  description: Create a production-ready Django service with database, CI/CD, and monitoring
  tags:
    - django
    - python
    - recommended
spec:
  owner: platform-team
  type: service
  
  # Parameters shown in the UI form
  parameters:
    - title: Service Information
      required:
        - name
        - owner
      properties:
        name:
          title: Service Name
          type: string
          description: Unique name for your service (lowercase, no spaces)
          pattern: '^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$'
          ui:field: EntityNamePicker
        owner:
          title: Owner Team
          type: string
          description: Team responsible for this service
          ui:field: OwnerPicker
          ui:options:
            catalogFilter:
              kind: Group
        description:
          title: Description
          type: string
          description: What does this service do?
    
    - title: Infrastructure Options
      properties:
        environment:
          title: Initial Environment
          type: string
          enum: ['staging', 'production']
          default: 'staging'
        database:
          title: Database Type
          type: string
          enum: ['postgresql', 'mysql', 'none']
          default: 'postgresql'
        databaseSize:
          title: Database Size
          type: string
          enum: ['small', 'medium', 'large']
          default: 'small'
          description: 'small=db.t3.micro, medium=db.r5.large, large=db.r5.xlarge'
        redis:
          title: Enable Redis Cache
          type: boolean
          default: true
  
  # Steps executed when developer clicks "Create"
  steps:
    # 1. Generate repository from cookiecutter template
    - id: fetch-template
      name: Fetch Django Template
      action: fetch:template
      input:
        url: ./skeleton
        values:
          name: ${{ parameters.name }}
          owner: ${{ parameters.owner }}
          description: ${{ parameters.description }}
    
    # 2. Create GitHub repository
    - id: publish-github
      name: Create GitHub Repository
      action: publish:github
      input:
        allowedHosts: ['github.com']
        repoUrl: github.com?owner=anihpj-org&repo=${{ parameters.name }}
        description: ${{ parameters.description }}
        defaultBranch: main
        protectDefaultBranch: true
    
    # 3. Create ArgoCD application for GitOps
    - id: create-argocd-app
      name: Setup GitOps Deployment
      action: argocd:create-application
      input:
        name: ${{ parameters.name }}
        namespace: ${{ parameters.environment }}
        repoUrl: ${{ steps['publish-github'].output.remoteUrl }}
        path: helm
    
    # 4. Provision database via Crossplane
    - id: create-database
      name: Provision Database
      action: kubernetes:apply
      input:
        manifest:
          apiVersion: database.crossplane.io/v1alpha1
          kind: PostgreSQLInstance
          metadata:
            name: ${{ parameters.name }}-db
          spec:
            size: ${{ parameters.databaseSize }}
            writeConnectionSecretToRef:
              name: ${{ parameters.name }}-db-credentials
    
    # 5. Register in Backstage catalog
    - id: register
      name: Register in Catalog
      action: catalog:register
      input:
        repoContentsUrl: ${{ steps['publish-github'].output.repoContentsUrl }}
        catalogInfoPath: '/catalog-info.yaml'
  
  output:
    links:
      - title: Repository
        url: ${{ steps['publish-github'].output.remoteUrl }}
      - title: ArgoCD
        url: https://argocd.internal/applications/${{ parameters.name }}
      - title: Open in Catalog
        icon: catalog
        entityRef: ${{ steps['register'].output.entityRef }}
🚀 backstage/catalog-info.yaml 
# Auto-generated catalog entry after template execution
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  description: Main Django application for ANIHPJ project
  annotations:
    github.com/project-slug: anihpj-org/anihpj
    argocd/app-name: anihpj
    backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.
  tags:
    - django
    - python
    - production
  links:
    - url: https://anihpj.com
      title: Production
    - url: https://staging.anihpj.com
      title: Staging
spec:
  type: service
  lifecycle: production
  owner: platform-team
  system: anihpj-platform
  dependsOn:
    - resource:default/anihpj-db
    - resource:default/anihpj-redis
  providesApis:
    - anihpj-api
▶️ Developer Experience at Level 4
# At Level 4, developers DON'T run commands!
# They use a web UI:

# 1. Go to https://backstage.internal
# 2. Click "Create" → "Django Service"
# 3. Fill out the form:
#    - Name: my-new-service
#    - Owner: backend-team
#    - Database: postgresql (medium)
#    - Redis: enabled
# 4. Click "Create"
# 5. Wait 3 minutes...
# 6. Service is live with:
#    - GitHub repo with CI/CD
#    - Database provisioned
#    - Deployed to staging
#    - Monitoring configured
#    - Documentation site

# The platform abstracts ALL complexity!

Your new service "my-new-service" is ready:

   https://github.com/anihpj-org/my-new-service

🚀 Environments
   Staging: https://my-new-service.staging.internal
   Production: Ready for promotion

🗄️ Database
   Host: my-new-service-db.xxxxx.rds.amazonaws.com
   Credentials: Stored in AWS Secrets Manager

📊 Monitoring
   Dashboard: https://grafana.internal/d/my-new-service
   Alerts: Configured (5xx errors, latency > 500ms)

📚 Documentation
   TechDocs: https://backstage.internal/docs/my-new-service

Next Steps:
1. Clone the repository
2. Run 'make dev' to start local development
3. Push to main to deploy to staging
Example 5: Evolution Path - Growing Up the Pyramid Scenario: How a backup solution evolves through all 4 levels over time
📁 Evolution Timeline
📋 backup-evolution.md 
# Backup Solution Evolution Through the Pyramid

## Month 1: Level 1 (Script)
"We need backups NOW"

```bash
#!/bin/bash
pg_dump $DATABASE_URL | gzip > /backups/db_$(date +%Y%m%d).sql.gz
```
- Developer runs manually when they remember
- Sometimes forgets for weeks
- No verification, no retention policy


## Month 3: Level 2 (SDK/Library)
"We need more control and error handling"

```python
import boto3
import subprocess
from datetime import datetime

def backup():
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    result = subprocess.run(['pg_dump', os.environ['DATABASE_URL']], 
                          capture_output=True, check=True)
    s3.put_object(Bucket='backups', Key=f'db_{datetime.now()}.sql', 
                  Body=result.stdout)
```
- Added S3 upload, error handling
- Still manual execution
- Better but not automated


## Month 6: Level 3 (Tools)  
"We need this to work across all our databases"

```yaml
# ansible/roles/backup/tasks/main.yml
- name: Backup PostgreSQL database
  community.postgresql.postgresql_dump:
    db: "{{ db_name }}"
    dump_extra_args: "--format=custom"
  register: dump_result

- name: Upload to S3
  amazon.aws.s3_object:
    bucket: "{{ backup_bucket }}"
    object: "{{ db_name }}/{{ ansible_date_time.date }}.dump"
    src: "{{ dump_result.path }}"
    mode: put
    
- name: Cleanup old backups
  amazon.aws.s3_object:
    bucket: "{{ backup_bucket }}"
    prefix: "{{ db_name }}/"
    mode: delobj
  when: object.last_modified < (now() - timedelta(days=retention_days))
```
- Works across all databases
- Scheduled via cron/systemd
- Retention policy enforced


## Month 12: Level 4 (Platform)
"Developers should self-service their backup needs"

Database team provides a Backstage template.
Developers just check a box: ☑️ Enable automated backups

Behind the scenes:
1. Crossplane provisions Kubernetes CronJob
2. Velero handles backup execution  
3. Prometheus monitors backup success
4. PagerDuty alerts on failures
5. Compliance dashboard shows backup status

# Key Insight: You don't SKIP levels!
# Each level builds on the previous
# Scripts inform SDK usage
# SDK experience shapes tool selection  
# Tool expertise enables platform design
📊 pyramid-metrics.yaml 
# Metrics at each pyramid level

level_1_scripts:
  time_to_implement: "30 minutes"
  maintenance_burden: "High (manual runs, no monitoring)"
  reliability: "Low (depends on human memory)"
  scalability: "None (single database)"
  best_for: "Proof of concept, emergency fixes"

level_2_sdk:
  time_to_implement: "4 hours"
  maintenance_burden: "Medium (code updates needed)"
  reliability: "Medium (error handling, but manual)"
  scalability: "Limited (requires code changes)"
  best_for: "Custom integrations, complex logic"

level_3_tools:
  time_to_implement: "2 days"
  maintenance_burden: "Low (declarative, versioned)"
  reliability: "High (scheduled, monitored)"
  scalability: "Good (parameterized for reuse)"
  best_for: "Team standardization, repeatability"

level_4_platform:
  time_to_implement: "2 weeks (initial), 5 minutes (per use)"
  maintenance_burden: "Very Low (self-service)"
  reliability: "Very High (automated, self-healing)"
  scalability: "Excellent (organization-wide)"
  best_for: "Enterprise standardization, developer experience"

# ROI Calculation
roi_analysis:
  startup_phase: "Stay at Level 1-2, move fast"
  growth_phase: "Invest in Level 3 tools"
  enterprise_phase: "Build Level 4 platforms"
  
  rule_of_thumb: |
    If you're doing something:
    - Once → Script it (Level 1)
    - Weekly → Codify it (Level 2)  
    - Daily → Tool it (Level 3)
    - Per team/project → Platform it (Level 4)
💡 Key Takeaways
The Automation Pyramid teaches us:

1. START AT THE BASE
   → Always start with scripts to understand the problem
   → Don't over-engineer from day one

2. CLIMB WHEN PAIN EXCEEDS INVESTMENT
   → Script becomes unmaintainable? Move to SDK
   → SDK used across many projects? Create a tool
   → Tool used by many teams? Build a platform

3. EACH LEVEL ABSTRACTS THE PREVIOUS
   → SDK abstracts shell commands
   → Tools abstract SDK calls
   → Platforms abstract tool configurations

4. VALUE INCREASES WITH HEIGHT
   → More reusability
   → More standardization
   → Less cognitive load for users

5. DON'T SKIP LEVELS
   → You need script experience to build good tools
   → You need tool experience to design good platforms

As a DevOps engineer, you should be comfortable at ALL levels:
- Junior: Mostly Level 1-2
- Mid: Mostly Level 2-3
- Senior: Mostly Level 3-4
- Staff/Principal: Designing Level 4 platforms

1.3 Why Scripting Matters for DevOps/Cloud Engineers

Career Critical Beginner

Understanding why scripting is a non-negotiable skill for modern DevOps and Cloud roles

Comprehensive Overview

Scripting is the foundational skill that separates effective DevOps engineers from those who just click buttons in cloud consoles. Every major DevOps responsibility—from CI/CD pipelines to infrastructure automation to incident response—requires scripting ability.

In today's cloud-native world, manual operations don't scale. A DevOps engineer who can't script is like a chef who can't use a knife. You might get by for simple tasks, but you'll never be truly effective.

DevOps Task Without Scripting With Scripting
Deploy to 50 servers 4 hours (manual SSH) 5 minutes (automated)
Analyze 1GB of logs Impossible manually 30 seconds (grep/awk)
Rotate secrets monthly Error-prone checklist Automated + verified
Scale during traffic spike Scramble to console Auto-triggered script
Incident response at 3 AM 30 min to fully wake up Script runs instantly
Example 1: Real Time Savings - Server Configuration Scenario: Configure Nginx on 20 servers - manual vs scripted approach
📁 Project Context Files
📄 infrastructure-overview 
Production Infrastructure
├── Load Balancer (ALB)
├── Web Tier (20 EC2 instances)
│   ├── web-001.anihpj.internal
│   ├── web-002.anihpj.internal
│   ├── ...
│   └── web-020.anihpj.internal
├── App Tier (10 EC2 instances)
└── Database (RDS PostgreSQL)

Task: Update Nginx configuration on all 20 web servers
- Add new cache headers
- Update SSL certificate
- Restart Nginx
⛔ manual-approach.txt DON'T DO THIS 
# Manual approach - what you'd do WITHOUT scripting skills

For each server (repeat 20 times):
1. SSH into server: ssh web-001.anihpj.internal
2. Open file: sudo vim /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
3. Find the location block (scroll, search...)
4. Add cache headers (type carefully, hope no typos)
5. Save file (:wq)
6. Copy SSL cert: sudo cp ~/new-cert.pem /etc/ssl/
7. Test config: sudo nginx -t
8. Restart: sudo systemctl restart nginx
9. Verify: curl -I http://localhost
10. Exit and move to next server

Time per server: ~10 minutes (if no mistakes)
Total time: 20 × 10 = 200 minutes = 3.3 hours
Error rate: High (typos, missed servers, inconsistencies)
Audit trail: None
